name: Liechtenstein (Principality of Liechtenstein / Fürstentum Liechtenstein)
summary: Liechtenstein is a Central European civil-law constitutional monarchy whose family-law framework operates under the General Civil Code (Allgemeines Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch, ABGB — adopted from Austria with substantial modernisation) supplemented by the Children and Youth Act 2008 (Kinder- und Jugendgesetz). Parental responsibility (Obsorge) and child custody are governed by ABGB §§ 137-186. The Supreme Court of Liechtenstein (Oberster Gerichtshof) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters; the Constitutional Court (Staatsgerichtshof) operates separate constitutional review. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in the Princely Court (Fürstliches Landgericht). Psychology profession is regulated through the Liechtenstein Office of Public Health (Amt für Gesundheit) framework. Liechtenstein is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the child's-best-interests standard. Liechtenstein acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 February 1998. Liechtenstein is a Council of Europe member subject to ECHR jurisdiction.

statutory_framework.title: General Civil Code — Parental responsibility (Obsorge)
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal civil code adopted from Austria with substantial modernisation. §§ 137-186 govern parental responsibility and child custody.

statutory_framework.citation: Children and Youth Act 2008 (Kinder- und Jugendgesetz)
statutory_framework.title: Children and Youth Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Children and Youth Act codifying child welfare provisions aligned with UNCRC obligations.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court (Oberster Gerichtshof)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

apex_courts.name: Constitutional Court (Staatsgerichtshof)
apex_courts.role: Constitutional Court with original jurisdiction over constitutional review.

professional_regulators.name: Office of Public Health (Amt für Gesundheit), Liechtenstein
professional_regulators.role: Federal regulator of health and allied health professionals including clinical psychology.

anonymisation_convention: Liechtenstein family-court decisions are anonymised per Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Principality of Liechtenstein established + Habsburg-imperial framework
key_developments.description: Principality of Liechtenstein established 23 January 1719 by Holy Roman Emperor Charles VI — substantively distinctive Central European Habsburg-imperial-framework. Foundational pre-Civil-Code framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Liechtenstein sovereignty + Confederation-of-the-Rhine framework
key_developments.description: Liechtenstein sovereignty 12 July 1806 via Confederation-of-the-Rhine framework — substantively distinctive Central European Napoleonic-era sovereignty framework. Foundational substantive-sovereignty framework persisting through Holy-Roman-Empire-dissolution and subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory.

key_developments.title: ABGB adoption from Austria
key_developments.description: General Civil Code (ABGB) adopted from Austria 1 June 1812 — establishing Austrian-civil-law substantive heritage. §§ 137-186 govern parental responsibility and child custody. Foundational substantive-civil-law framework persisting through subsequent reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Constitution 1921 + constitutional-monarchy framework
key_developments.description: Constitution of Liechtenstein 1921 adopted 5 October 1921 substantively reforming political-institutional framework establishing constitutional-monarchy framework. Substantive constitutional anchor for contemporary family-law jurisprudence persisting through subsequent reforms 2003.

key_developments.title: Council of Europe membership
key_developments.description: Liechtenstein joined the Council of Europe 23 November 1978; ECHR became applicable. Substantively significant Central European human-rights-framework integration affecting subsequent family-law-jurisprudence.

key_developments.title: Liechtenstein UN membership + UNCRC ratification framework
key_developments.description: Liechtenstein UN admission 18 September 1990 — substantively significant micro-state-UN-membership framework. Liechtenstein ratified the UNCRC 22 December 1995 — substantively significant child-rights framework integration affecting subsequent family-law-modernisation framework.

key_developments.title: EEA accession + EU-Single-Market-integration framework
key_developments.description: Liechtenstein European Economic Area membership 1 May 1995 — substantively distinctive globally micro-state-EEA-membership framework. Substantive EU-Single-Market integration affecting subsequent cross-border-jurisdiction-practice including Brussels-IIbis-Regulation-equivalent framework.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession
key_developments.description: Liechtenstein acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 February 1998 — substantively significant Central European Hague-accession framework affecting cross-border-jurisdiction-practice.

key_developments.title: Constitution 2003 + monarchical-powers-expansion framework
key_developments.description: Constitution-amendments March 2003 by referendum substantively reforming political-institutional framework — substantive monarchical-powers-expansion framework controversially distinctive globally pro-monarchical-reform pattern. Substantive constitutional-democratic-trajectory affecting subsequent family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: EEA framework continuation + Verein für Menschenrechte Liechtenstein + Schweiz-Liechtenstein cross-border family-law framework
key_developments.description: Liechtenstein EEA membership framework continuation through 2023 substantively significant for EU-acquis family-law harmonisation. Verein für Menschenrechte in Liechtenstein institutional position addressing PA-related issues within ABGB + Children and Youth Act 2008 framework. Schweiz-Liechtenstein cross-border family-law framework substantively significant for Customs Union cross-border family-law adjudication. Substantively significant Alpine micro-state institutional jurisprudential development.

key_developments.title: Oberster Gerichtshof + Staatsgerichtshof — child's-best-interests substantive register + Hans-Adam-II/Alois continuing-reign
key_developments.description: Oberster Gerichtshof and Staatsgerichtshof continue to develop child's-best-interests jurisprudence under ABGB §§ 137-186 + Children and Youth Act 2008 + Hague Convention 1980 framework in custody disputes within Hans-Adam-II reign since 1989 and Hereditary Prince Alois regency since 2004. Substantive analysis without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label adoption.

structural_findings: Liechtenstein operates an Austrian-ABGB-derivative civil-law family-law framework — places Liechtenstein in the German-speaking civil-law cluster with Austria and Germany within the corpus.
Council of Europe + ECHR membership places Liechtenstein in the ECHR-Hague intersection cluster.
Hague Convention 1980 accession 1998 places Liechtenstein in the Hague Central European cluster.
Micro-state status (~38,000 population, ~160 km²) is structurally distinctive within the corpus.
editorial_notes: Liechtenstein jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-09 from 4 to 10 key_developments with full Principality-1719-to-Alois-regency trajectory: 1719-Principality-of-Liechtenstein-+-Habsburg + 1806-Liechtenstein-sovereignty-+-Confederation-of-the-Rhine + 1812-ABGB-adoption-from-Austria + 1921-Constitution-+-constitutional-monarchy + 1978-Council-of-Europe-membership + 1990-Liechtenstein-UN-membership-+-UNCRC + 1995-EEA-accession-+-EU-Single-Market + 1998-Hague-Convention-1980-accession + 2003-Constitution-amendments-+-monarchical-powers-expansion + 2024-Oberster-Gerichtshof-+-Staatsgerichtshof-+-Hans-Adam-II-Alois.
Civil-law Central European micro-state (ABGB Austrian-derivative + Children and Youth Act 2008 + ECHR + Hague Convention 1980 accession 1998).

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive child's-best-interests analysis under ABGB §§ 137-186 + Children and Youth Act 2008 + Hague Convention 1980 framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins Central-European + civil-law + Austrian-ABGB-derivative (with Austria) + ECHR + Hague-Convention + micro-state-38000-population-160-km2-distinctive + Principality-1719-Habsburg-imperial-Charles-VI + Confederation-of-the-Rhine-1806-sovereignty + Constitution-1921 + EEA-membership-1995-micro-state-EU-Single-Market-distinctive + Constitution-amendments-2003-monarchical-powers-expansion-globally-controversially-distinctive-pro-monarchical-reform + Hans-Adam-II-reign-1989-2026-+-Hereditary-Prince-Alois-regency-2004 clusters within the corpus.
